quotes.text: WITH no selfish or partial joy ... they rejoiced.
IF thou see others lamenting, join in their lamentations: if thou hear others rejoicing, join in their joy.
My son, tell me thy sorrow, that it may become more endurable by participation.
LET the wise man guard his thoughts, for they are ... very artful and rush wheresoever they list.
To think no evil and do none: on the contrary, to benefit all creatures.
EVERY variety of living creature I must ever defend from harm.
WHEN thou seest righteousness, quickly follow it: when thou seest iniquity, instantly flee.
DISGUSTED ... with all sins.
MAY I never, even in a dream, be guilty of theft, adultery, drunkenness, life-slaughter, and untruthfulness.
SPOTLESS even as the moon, pure, serene, and undisturbed.
PRACTISE the most perfect virtue.
TO attain perfection that he may profit others.
THE present is an imperfect existence: ... I pray for greater perfection in the next.
quotes.source: Inscription in Temple of Nakhon Vat

quotes.text: FULFIL the perfection of long-suffering; be thou patient under ... reproach.
MY duty is to bear all the insults which the heretics launch against me.
SILENTLY shall I endure abuse, as the elephant in battle endures the arrow sent from the bow.
LET not the member of Buddha’s order tremble at blame, neither let him puff himself up when praised.
CULTIVATE equanimity.

THERE are two extremes ... which he who has given up the world should avoid— ... a life devoted to pleasures: ... this is degrading, sensual, ... ignoble; ... and a life given to mortifications: this is painful ... and profitless.
THE end of the pleasures of sense is as the lightning flash: ... what profit, then, in doing iniquity?
HE speaks truth unmixed with falsehood.
THERE is (guilt calling for repentance) in prevarication.
HE that praises him who should be blamed, or blames him who should be praised, gathers up sin thereby in his mouth.
IN four ways may the flatterer be known a false friend – he assents when you do wrong; he assents when you do right; he sounds your praises before your face, and speaks ill of you behind your back.
THE member of Buddha’s order ... should abstain ... from theft, even of a blade of grass.
FROM bribery, cheating, fraud, and (all other) crooked ways he abstains.
THE Scripture moveth us, therefore, rather to cut off the hand than to take anything which is not ours.
ALL the people were bound close in family love and friendship.
I GIVE up my own will, and live only according to the will of these ... brethren.
THENCEFORTH he devoted himself to caring for others alone.
quotes.source: Questions of King Milinda (Book 4, ch. 1, sec. 37)

quotes.text: EVEN as the lily lives upon and loves the water, So Upatissa and Kolita likewise, Joined by closest bond of love, If by necessity compelled to live apart, Were overcome by grief and aching heart.
(THE true friend) forsakes you not in trouble; he will lay down his life for your sake.

IN grief as well as in joy we are united, In sorrow and in happiness alike. * * * * That which your heart rejoices in as good, That I also rejoice in and follow. It were better I should die with you, Than ... attempt to live where you are not.
LET not the member of Buddha's order be a boaster.
GLORY not in thyself, but rather in thy neighbour.
